name: Creation
summary: God creates the heavens, the earth, and all living things, declaring creation good.
longDescription:
Genesis opens with God bringing order, light, life, and humanity into existence by his word. The account presents creation as purposeful, structured, and good.

Humanity is made in God's image and given responsibility over the created world. The seventh day is blessed as a day of rest, setting a rhythm that echoes through Scripture.
historicalNotes: The account speaks into an ancient Near Eastern world but presents one sovereign Creator rather than competing deities.

name: The Fall
summary: Adam and Eve disobey God, bringing sin, death, and exile from Eden into human history.
longDescription:
The man and woman reject God's command and eat from the tree of the knowledge of good and evil. Their disobedience fractures fellowship with God, one another, and creation.

God judges the serpent, the woman, and the man, yet the passage also contains hope that the offspring of the woman will defeat the serpent.

name: Cain and Abel
summary: Cain murders Abel after God accepts Abel's offering and warns Cain about sin.
longDescription:
Genesis 4 traces sin's spread from disobedience to violence. Cain becomes angry when God regards Abel's offering and not his own.

God warns Cain that sin is crouching at the door, but Cain kills his brother. Cain is judged and sent out, yet God protects him from vengeance.

name: The Flood
summary: God judges widespread human corruption with a flood and preserves Noah's family through the ark.
longDescription:
Human violence and corruption fill the earth, and God announces judgment through a great flood. Noah is described as righteous in his generation and obeys God's command to build the ark.

After the flood, God establishes a covenant with Noah, promising not to destroy all flesh by flood again and giving the rainbow as the sign.
historicalNotes: Flood traditions appear in several ancient Near Eastern texts, though Genesis gives the event a distinct covenantal and moral focus.

name: Tower of Babel
summary: Humanity gathers to build a city and tower, and God confuses their language and scatters them.
longDescription:
The people of Babel seek to make a name for themselves and avoid being scattered across the earth. Their project becomes a symbol of unified human pride apart from God.

God confuses their language, halting the project and scattering the peoples. The narrative prepares for God's call of Abram and the promise to bless all nations.
historicalNotes: Babel is associated with Babylon and the Mesopotamian world, where temple towers known as ziggurats were prominent.

name: Call of Abraham
summary: God calls Abram to leave his country and promises land, descendants, and blessing to all families of the earth.
longDescription:
Abram is called from his homeland to a land God will show him. The promises of land, great nationhood, blessing, and worldwide blessing become a central thread in biblical history.

Abram obeys and travels to Canaan, building altars as he moves through the land. The patriarchal narrative develops God's covenant faithfulness through his family.
historicalNotes: Traditional chronology places Abraham in the early second millennium BC, though exact dating is debated.

name: Birth of Isaac
summary: Isaac is born to Abraham and Sarah as the promised child through whom the covenant line continues.
longDescription:
God keeps his promise to Abraham and Sarah by giving them a son in their old age. Isaac's birth demonstrates that the covenant family exists because of God's faithfulness, not human strength.

The name Isaac, connected with laughter, recalls both the seeming impossibility of the promise and the joy of its fulfillment.

name: Jacob and Esau
summary: Jacob receives the birthright and blessing, continuing the covenant line through a troubled family story.
longDescription:
Jacob and Esau struggle from birth, and their rivalry shapes the next generation of the patriarchal family. Esau despises his birthright, and Jacob later receives Isaac's blessing through deception.

Despite human conflict and sin, the covenant promise continues through Jacob, who will later be named Israel.

name: Joseph in Egypt
summary: Joseph is sold into Egypt, rises to authority, and preserves his family during famine.
longDescription:
Joseph's brothers sell him into slavery, but God works through suffering, false accusation, and imprisonment to bring Joseph before Pharaoh. Joseph interprets dreams and becomes a ruler in Egypt.

During famine, Joseph reconciles with his brothers and provides for Jacob's family. Genesis closes with Israel's family in Egypt and with hope that God will bring them back to the promised land.
historicalNotes: The Joseph narratives fit a setting of movement between Canaan and Egypt during periods when famine made Egypt a refuge.

name: Exodus from Egypt
summary: God delivers Israel from slavery in Egypt through Moses after the Passover judgment.
longDescription:
After generations in Egypt, Israel is enslaved and cries out to God. Through Moses, God confronts Pharaoh with signs and judgments, culminating in the Passover.

Israel leaves Egypt as a redeemed people, beginning the journey toward Sinai and the land promised to Abraham.
historicalNotes: Traditional early-date chronology places the exodus in the fifteenth century BC, while other chronologies propose later dates.

name: The Ten Commandments
summary: God gives Israel the covenant law at Sinai, beginning with the Ten Commandments.
longDescription:
At Mount Sinai, God speaks the words of the covenant to Israel. The commandments define worship of the Lord and justice toward neighbor for the redeemed people.

The law follows deliverance, showing Israel how to live as God's covenant nation after being brought out of Egypt.

name: David's Reign
summary: David rules Israel from Jerusalem and receives the covenant promise of an enduring dynasty.
longDescription:
David is anointed king over all Israel and establishes Jerusalem as his capital. His reign marks the high point of the united monarchy and shapes Israel's worship, politics, and hope.

In 2 Samuel 7, God promises David a house and kingdom, creating the expectation of a future Davidic king.
historicalNotes: David is commonly placed in the early tenth century BC. Archaeological discussions continue around the scale of the united monarchy.
